questions.question: List and explain any three categories of software myths with one example for each.
keyPoints: Management myths - beliefs held by managers (e.g., adding programmers speeds up late projects)
Customer myths - misconceptions by clients (e.g., requirements won't change)
Practitioner myths - false beliefs by developers (e.g., testing can catch all defects)

questions.question: What is Brooks' Law? Explain with reference to the 'add more programmers' myth.
keyPoints: Brooks' Law states adding people to a late project makes it later
New programmers require ramp-up time and increase communication overhead
Explains why adding resources doesn't solve schedule problems

questions.question: Explain the Iron Triangle concept in project management and its relevance to software development.
keyPoints: Iron Triangle = Scope, Time, Cost tradeoff
Changing one element affects the other two
In software, scope changes impact schedule and budget

questions.question: Differentiate between management myths and practitioner myths in software development.
keyPoints: Management myths are held by project managers and executives
Practitioner myths are held by developers and technical staff
Examples of each category with clear distinctions

questions.question: Why is the 'quality can be tested into software' myth dangerous? Suggest a better approach.
keyPoints: Testing can only find defects, not prevent them
Quality must be built in from the start
Better approach: Quality assurance throughout development

questions.question: What is 'wishful thinking' in software estimation? Give examples of how it manifests.
keyPoints: Making estimates based on desired outcomes not reality
Ignoring historical data and complexity analysis
Examples: optimistic deadlines, underestimating effort

questions.question: Explain why the 'programmers are interchangeable' myth is false.
keyPoints: Each programmer has tacit knowledge and domain expertise
Familiarity with specific codebase varies
Communication and collaboration patterns matter

questions.question: Discuss the common software myths related to requirements and explain why 'requirements can be frozen' is an unrealistic expectation in practice.
keyPoints: Myths about requirements stability and completeness
Reality: requirements evolve throughout project
Impact of changing requirements on schedule and cost
Need for change management processes
Examples from real projects

questions.question: Explain Brooks' Law and discuss how the 'add more programmers to a late project' myth has caused failures in software projects. Provide recommendations to avoid this pitfall.
keyPoints: Statement and explanation of Brooks' Law
Reasons why adding resources doesn't help
Communication overhead increases exponentially
Ramp-up time for new team members
Recommendations: proper planning, early detection, incremental delivery

questions.question: Analyze the various categories of software myths and explain with examples how each category contributes to project failure.
keyPoints: Management myths and their impact
Customer myths and unrealistic expectations
Practitioner myths affecting quality
Case studies or examples of failures
Mitigation strategies for each category

questions.question: What is the Iron Triangle? How does understanding this concept help in managing software projects and avoiding common myths?
keyPoints: Definition of Iron Triangle (Scope, Time, Cost)
Tradeoffs between the three elements
How understanding this avoids schedule and scope myths
Real-world examples of project failures due to ignoring Iron Triangle
Strategies for balanced project management
